question: How do students join the Office Hours or live workshop if the Zoom link isn’t public?

Students join via YouTube Live, not the public Zoom link. The Zoom link is only shared with instructors/presenters/TAs.

They can:
- Watch live on the DataTalksClub YouTube channel
- Use the video URL posted in the announcements channel on Telegram and Slack before the session starts
- Ask questions in Slido, whose link is pinned in the live chat

Don’t post questions in the chat, since they may be missed.

question: How do I find my own entry on the leaderboard if I can’t see my name right away?
answer_llm: Click the **“Jump to your record on the leaderboard”** link — it will take you directly to your entry. If you want to see the name shown there, use **“Edit Course Profile”** to check your display name.
answer_orig:
When you set up your account, you are automatically assigned a random name, such as “Lucid Elbakyan.” Click on the "Jump to your record on the leaderboard" link to find your entry.

If you want to see what your Display name is, click on the "Edit Course Profile" button.

<{IMAGE:image_1}>

- **First field:** This is your nickname/displayed name. You can change it if you want to be known by your Slack username, GitHub username, or any other nickname of your choice. This is useful if you want to remain anonymous.
- **Second field:** Change this to your official name as in your identification documents—passport, national ID card, driver's license, etc. This is mandatory if you do not want "Lucid Elbakyan" on your certificate. This name will appear on your Certificate!

question: Why do I show up with a random name on the leaderboard instead of my actual name?
answer_llm:
Because the leaderboard uses your **public display name**, which is set separately from the name used on your certificate. By default, that display name is a **random anonymous nickname** like “Lucid Elbakyan.”

If you want to change it, go to **Edit Course Profile**:
- **First field:** leaderboard nickname/display name
- **Second field:** certificate name

If you don’t change the first field, the random name will stay on the leaderboard.

question: Why does the homework content keep changing before the deadline?
answer_llm:
Because the homework may still be in draft status. If the title contains `[DRAFT]`, the homework is not ready yet, and its content can still change until both of these are true:

- the homework form is open on the course management platform
- the homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`

Working on it before that is at your own risk, since the final version may differ.
answer_orig:
If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means the homework is not ready yet.

The homework is ready only when both are true:

- The homework form is open on the course management platform.
- The homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, the content can still change. Working on the material or homework in advance is at your own risk, because the final version can be different.

question: Why do I get an OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits when I run responses.create?
answer_llm:
Because your OpenAI account doesn’t have enough API credits/quota.

This error means `responses.create()` is being blocked by **insufficient quota**, not a code issue. There is no free OpenAI API usage, so you need to:

1. Add credits to your OpenAI account (pay-as-you-go, minimum $5).
2. Make sure you’re using a model available to your account.
3. If needed, create a new API key after adding credits and update it locally.

If you want, I can also help you check whether you’re using the right model or endpoint.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: Is the responses.create command failing because my OpenAI account has no credits left?
answer_llm:
Yes — if you’re seeing an `OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits` or a `429 insufficient_quota` error, it’s because your OpenAI API account has run out of credits or quota.

To fix it:
1. Add credits to your OpenAI account.
2. Make sure you’re using a model available to your account.
3. You may need to create a new API key after adding credits.

If you want, I can also help you distinguish between an OpenAI quota issue and an OpenRouter `402` issue.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: What does the insufficient credits error mean in OpenAI responses.create?
answer_llm: It means your OpenAI API account doesn’t have enough quota/credits to run `responses.create`. You need to add billing credits to your account and then try again.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: How can I fix the error I see when using OpenAI responses.create?
answer_llm:
The error is usually due to **insufficient OpenAI credits / quota**.

To fix it:

1. **Add credits to your OpenAI account**  
   Go to the billing page and add at least $5:  
   https://platform.openai.com/settings/organization/billing/overview

question: Why am I getting a 429 RateLimitError from OpenAI even though my code looks fine?
answer_llm:
A 429 from OpenAI doesn’t always mean your code is wrong. In the course context, it often means **your account has run out of quota / billing credit**.

If the error says something like:

- `You exceeded your current quota`
- `type: 'insufficient_quota'`

then it’s a **billing issue**, not a bug in your code.

What to do:
1. Add credits to your OpenAI account (pay-as-you-go, minimum about $5).
2. Make sure you’re using a model available to your account.
3. You may need to create a new API key after adding credits and update it locally.

If you’re just hitting normal rate limits during the course, you can also:
- use fewer API calls,
- reduce concurrency,
- add retries with backoff,
- cache results.

But if the error is `insufficient_quota`, the fix is billing-related.

For more information on this error, read the docs: [https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/error-codes/api-errors.](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/error-codes/api-errors.)', 'type': 'insufficient_quota', 'param': None, 'code': 'insufficient_quota'}

question: Why does the course recommend uv over pip and virtualenv for setting up Python projects?
answer_llm:
The course recommends **uv** because it’s **fast and convenient**. It’s an **all-in-one tool** that can replace several traditional tools, including **pip**, **virtualenv**, **pip-tools**, **pipx**, **poetry**, **pyenv**, and **twine**.

So instead of juggling multiple tools for:
- Python versions
- virtual environments
- dependency management
- reproducible lockfiles

you can handle everything with one fast command.

That said, **you don’t have to use uv** — **pip and virtualenv still work fine**. The course uses uv mainly because it makes the setup **simpler and faster**.

The course uses uv because it's fast and convenient. It's an all-in-one tool that replaces pip, virtualenv, pip-tools, pipx, poetry, pyenv, and twine — instead of juggling several tools, you manage Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies through one fast command with reproducible lockfiles.

You're not required to use it — pip and virtualenv still work fine — but uv makes the setup simpler and faster, which is why the lessons use it.

Official docs: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/

question: How do I keep my API keys in a .env file and read them in Python without hardcoding them?
answer_llm:
Store the keys in a `.env` file, add `.env` to `.gitignore`, and load it in Python with `python-dotenv`.

Example:

**.env**
```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
GROQ_API_KEY=gsk_...
GEMINI_API_KEY=...
```

**Install**
```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

**Python**
```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

openai_api_key = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
groq_api_key = os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY")
```

Also make sure your repo ignores the file:

```gitignore
.env
```

If you want, I can also show you how to use a `.env.example` file for sharing the project safely.

question: How can I use Google Gemini in the Module 1 notebook if I only have an OpenAI-style client?
answer_llm:
Use the OpenAI client with Google’s OpenAI-compatible Gemini endpoint.

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

def llm(instructions, user_prompt, model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite"):
    messages = [
        {"role": "developer", "content": instructions},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}
    ]

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=messages
    )

    return response.choices[0].message.content
```

So the changes you need are:
- put your Gemini key in `.env` as `GEMINI_API_KEY`
- set `base_url` to Google’s OpenAI-compatible URL
- use a Gemini model name like `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`

Note that this uses `chat.completions` style, not the newer Responses API, so you may need to adapt notebook code where it reads outputs or handles tools/function calls.

question: Why does the Module 1 code need changes when switching from the newer Responses API to Gemini’s chat completions?
answer_llm:
Because the newer **Responses API** and **Gemini’s chat completions endpoint** are different interfaces.

In Module 1, the notebook code was written for the Responses API, so it may use things like:

- `client.responses.create(...)`
- `response.output`
- `responses.parse(...)`

But when you switch to Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint, you need to use the **chat-completions flow** instead:

- `client.chat.completions.create(...)`
- `response.choices[0].message.content`
- or `client.chat.completions.parse(...)` for structured output

So the code needs changes because the response shape and parsing method are different, especially for:

- how you read the model output
- how you handle tools/function calls
- how you get structured/Pydantic output

In short: **the Responses API is OpenAI-specific, while Gemini works better through the older chat-completions-compatible interface.**

question: How do I get Pydantic-typed output from Gemini if `responses.parse` doesn't work with the OpenAI-compatible API?
answer_llm:
Use the OpenAI SDK’s **chat-completions parsing flow** instead of `responses.parse`.

Define a Pydantic model for the output, then call:

```python
client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=YourModel
)
```

Example:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from openai import OpenAI
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

This gives you the parsed Pydantic object directly, without manual JSON parsing.

question: How do I install Ollama on macOS, Windows, or Linux?
answer_llm:
To install Ollama, go to **https://ollama.com/download** and choose your OS:

- **macOS**: download the **.pkg** file and install it.
- **Windows**: download the **.msi** file and install it.
- **Linux**: run:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
```

After installing, you can test it with:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```

And verify the local server with:

```bash
curl http://localhost:11434
```

question: Why does my `tiktoken` count not match the token number OpenAI shows in the API response?
answer_llm:
Because `tiktoken` only counts the text you pass in, while OpenAI’s API also adds hidden chat-formatting tokens.

In particular:

- each chat message adds about 4 structural tokens
- the API adds 2 tokens to mark the start of assistant generation
- extra whitespace, newlines, or unusual Unicode can add a few more

So your visible prompt might be 320 tokens in `tiktoken`, but OpenAI may report 327 in the API response.
answer_orig:
When using `tiktoken.encode()` to count tokens in your prompt, you might see a difference compared to OpenAI’s API response. For instance, you might get 320 tokens, while OpenAI reports 327. This is due to internal tokens added by OpenAI’s chat formatting.

Here’s what happens:

- Each message in a `chat.completions.create()` call (e.g., `{role: "user", content: "..."}`) adds 4 structural tokens (role, content, separators).
- The API adds 2 tokens globally to mark the start of assistant response generation.
- Extra newlines, whitespace, or uncommon Unicode characters in your content may slightly increase the token count.

Thus, even if your visible text is 320 tokens, OpenAI may count 327 due to these internal additions.

question: How can I keep my API key loaded automatically in a project so I don't have to export it in every new terminal?
answer_llm:
Use a local env-file loader so the key is loaded automatically when you enter the project directory.

The recommended option in the context is **`dirdotenv`**:

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv
```

Then add this to your shell config (`~/.bashrc` or `~/.zshrc`):

```bash
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh
```

Inside your project, create a `.env` file:

```bash
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
```

And ignore it in Git:

```bash
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically whenever you `cd` into the project.

If you already use it, **`direnv`** is also fine.
answer_orig:
Use [`dirdotenv`](https://github.com/alexeygrigorev/dirdotenv). It is like `direnv`, but works with both `.env` and `.envrc`, and is more portable across shells and operating systems.

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add this line to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh

# inside your project:
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically when you `cd` into the project directory.

Important: always add `.env` and `.envrc` to `.gitignore` so keys never land on GitHub.

`direnv` is also fine if you already use it. In that case, create `.envrc`, add your exports there, and run `direnv allow`.

For GitHub Codespaces, use the built-in [Codespaces secrets](https://docs.github.com/en/codespaces/managing-your-codespaces/managing-your-account-specific-secrets-for-github-codespaces) instead of files in the repo.

For Python scripts, the equivalent is `python-dotenv`:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()  # loads .env from project root
```

For GitHub Codespaces, use the built-in [Codespaces secrets](https://docs.github.com/en/codespaces/managing-your-codespaces/managing-your-account-specific-secrets-for-github-codespaces) instead of files in the repo.

For Python scripts, the equivalent is `python-dotenv`:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()  # loads .env from project root
```

question: How do I decide the best field weights in minsearch without just guessing?
answer_llm:
Don’t guess—**evaluate different weight settings against a ground-truth set**.

A practical way to do it:

1. Create a small set of representative questions.
2. Label which documents should be retrieved for each question.
3. Run minsearch with different field weights.
4. Compare retrieval metrics like **hit rate, precision@k, recall@k, or MRR**.

For small projects, trial and error can work, but **systematic evaluation is the more reliable approach**.

question: What should I do before indexing my docs for RAG so retrieval works better?
answer_llm:
Before indexing your docs for RAG, make the data **clean, structured, and easy to chunk and retrieve**.

Common things to do:
- Remove noise like broken HTML, duplicate text, boilerplate, OCR errors, repeated headers/footers
- Preserve useful context like titles, section names, dates, page numbers, speaker names, and Q&A structure
- Store the text in a structured format if helpful, often JSON
- Chunk it so related context stays together
- Keep useful metadata for later filtering or ranking

The main goal is to improve retrieval accuracy, not just shorten the text.

question: In GitHub Codespaces, `uv` shows “Failed to hardlink files” during install — does that actually mean something broke?
answer_llm:
No. In GitHub Codespaces, `uv` can’t always hardlink files from its cache, so it shows that warning and then falls back to copying files. The package still installs.

If you want to suppress it for the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```
answer_orig:
No. This warning can happen in GitHub Codespaces when `uv` cannot hardlink files between the cache and the target environment.

The package still installs. `uv` falls back to copying files.

To suppress the warning for the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

See the `uv` documentation for more details: [https://docs.astral.sh/uv/](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/).

question: Why does WSL2 say the model needs more memory than is available, even though my PC has enough RAM?
answer_llm:
Your WSL2 instance is only allowed to use the memory configured for WSL, not all the RAM on your PC. So even if your computer has enough RAM overall, WSL2 may still report that the model needs more memory than is available.

To fix it, increase WSL’s memory limit by creating a `.wslconfig` file in:

`C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`

Example:

```ini
[wsl2]
memory=8GB
```

Then restart WSL:

```bash
wsl --shutdown
```

After that, you can check the available memory inside WSL with `free`.
answer_orig:
Your WSL2 is set to use Y.Y GiB, not all your computer memory. To allocate more RAM, follow these steps:

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file under your Windows user profile directory: `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`.

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL using the command:

   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

For more details, read [this article](https://www.aleksandrhovhannisyan.com/blog/limiting-memory-usage-in-wsl-2/).


3. Restart WSL using the command:


4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

question: My GitHub Codespace is stuck on Finishing up and never opens — what can I try first?
answer_llm:
Try this first:

1. Go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces)
2. Stop the codespace
3. Start it again

If it’s still stuck on “Finishing up,” try opening it in the browser instead of desktop VS Code, or switch browsers (Edge/Chrome/Brave).

If nothing works, the last resort is to delete the codespace and create a new one.
answer_orig:
These are usually GitHub Codespaces reliability issues rather than a problem with the course, so there's no single guaranteed fix — but the following workarounds resolve most cases.

**Codespace won't connect / stuck on "Finishing up":**

- Go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces), stop the codespace, and start it again.
- If it still won't connect, open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or try a different browser (Edge/Chrome/Brave).
- As a last resort, delete the codespace and create a new one.

**"My setup is all gone":**

- The repo in `/workspaces` persists across stop/start, but a **rebuild or a brand-new codespace** starts from a clean image, and system/global installs outside your project don't always survive. Reinstalling is quick with `uv` (`uv sync` / `uv add ...`).
- **Commit and push your work often** — uncommitted changes survive a stop/start but are lost if you delete or recreate the codespace.

If Codespaces keeps being flaky for you, consider running the course locally instead — see "[Can I run the course locally instead of Codespaces?](026_aa310de435_can-i-run-the-course-locally-instead-of-codespaces.md)".

question: Can I use a different LLM provider instead of OpenAI for the RAG module, or is the course locked to OpenAI?
answer_llm:
Yes — the course is **not locked to OpenAI**.

For the RAG module, you can use **OpenAI or another LLM provider** such as **Gemini, Groq, OpenRouter, Azure OpenAI, local models, or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint**. If you switch providers, you may need to adjust the `base_url`, API key, model name, and possibly response/tool schema details.

So the short answer is: **you can absolutely use a different LLM provider instead of OpenAI**.
answer_orig:
If the provider used in the course isn't available or is blocked in your region (or you simply prefer another one), you can use any other LLM provider — the course isn't tied to OpenAI. Just switch to something else:

- Hosted, OpenAI-compatible providers — e.g. Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral. The course code uses the OpenAI client, so you usually only need to change the `base_url`, the API key, and the model name.
- Open models via Hugging Face (e.g. Qwen, Llama) if you prefer hosted open-source models.
- Serve a model locally with [Ollama](https://ollama.com/), [vLLM](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm), LM Studio, or anything else — no external API call at all, so regional blocks don't apply and you don't need a paid key. Most of these also expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so the course code works with only a `base_url` change.
- Rent a GPU machine and serve the model there (e.g. with vLLM) if your own machine can't run the model you want. This gives you a private OpenAI-compatible endpoint to point the course code at — just remember to stop/delete the instance when you're done so you don't keep paying for it.
- A VPN also works if you just need to reach a provider that's blocked at the network level.

Anything with an OpenAI-compatible endpoint (or a locally served model) will work — pick whatever is available and convenient for you.

For a curated list of options, see [Awesome LLMs](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo, which collects OpenAI API alternatives and tools for running models locally.

question: How can I stop getting 429 errors from OpenAI when my notebook loops over lots of documents?
answer_llm:
To avoid 429 errors, try to **reduce API calls before handling errors after the fact**:

- **Use retries with backoff**
  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI
  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```
- **Lower concurrency** — if you use threads/processes, keep it small (about 2–3 workers).
- **Cache outputs** — save embeddings/LLM responses to disk and reuse them on reruns.
- **Batch requests** — send multiple texts in one call when possible instead of looping one by one.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider** — `gpt-4o-mini` or Groq’s free tier can help stay within limits.

If the error is actually `insufficient_quota`, that’s a **billing/quota issue**, not a rate-limit issue. In that case you need to add credits to your OpenAI account.
answer_orig:
Free-tier and low-spend OpenAI accounts have per-minute and per-day request limits that are easy to blow through when a notebook loops over many documents. Reduce the number of API calls before you worry about handling the error after the fact.

- **Retry with backoff.** The `openai` Python client retries automatically with exponential backoff; raise the limit so transient 429s resolve themselves instead of crashing the run:

  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI

  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```

- **Lower concurrency.** In a thread/process pool, keep the pool small (2-3 workers) so you stay under the per-minute cap. A larger pool finishes one batch fast and then fails on the next.
- **Cache results.** Write embeddings and LLM responses to disk (JSONL, pickle, or a vector DB) and reload on re-run. Re-executing a notebook should not re-call the API for inputs you already processed.
- **Batch where possible.** Group independent inputs into a single request (e.g. embed a list of texts in one call) instead of looping one-by-one.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider.** `gpt-4o-mini` is cheap enough for the whole course; Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) has generous per-minute limits via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

If you already see `insufficient_quota`, that is a billing issue, not a rate limit — see [OpenAI: Error: RateLimitError: Error code: 429](openai-error-ratelimiterror-error-code-429).

- **Use a cheaper/free provider.** `gpt-4o-mini` is cheap enough for the whole course; Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) has generous per-minute limits via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

If you already see `insufficient_quota`, that is a billing issue, not a rate limit — see [OpenAI: Error: RateLimitError: Error code: 429](openai-error-ratelimiterror-error-code-429).

question: In agentic RAG, what exactly counts as a tool or function, and why do people use both words?
answer_llm:
In agentic RAG, a **tool/function** is anything the model can call to get external help, for example:

- searching documents
- querying a database
- calling an API
- running a calculation

The course treats **tools** and **functions** as very closely related terms, so you don’t need to worry much about the naming difference.

People use both words because different model providers and libraries use different terminology or schemas, but the core idea is the same: the model can decide **when** to call that external capability. In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a fixed step before the answer; in agentic RAG, the model can choose to invoke the tool/function itself.

question: Why do I get `AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'` when I call `responses.create(...)` with a non-OpenAI model?
answer_llm:
Because `responses.create(...)` uses OpenAI’s **Responses API**, and that API is **not implemented by non-OpenAI providers** like Mistral, Groq, or Gemini. With those providers, the call doesn’t return an OpenAI `response` object with `.output`; instead you may just get a plain string or a different response shape, so accessing `.output` raises:

`AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'`

Use the **chat-completions API** for non-OpenAI models instead:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,
)

return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You may also need to adapt the tools schema to match the provider’s expected format.
answer_orig:
The new OpenAI Responses API (`client.responses.create(...)`, accessed via `response.output`) is OpenAI-specific. Other providers (Mistral, Groq, Gemini, etc.) don't implement it.

For non-OpenAI providers, use the chat-completions API and read `response.choices[0].message.content`:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,  # may need adapting per provider
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You'll also have to adapt the tools schema to whatever shape your provider expects.

It is okay to use the older `chat.completions` API for homework or projects if your provider supports that interface better than the Responses API.

question: I'm getting Error 400 on Azure OpenAI saying tools[0].function is missing — what exactly should my weather tool JSON look like?
How do I fix the Azure OpenAI request when it complains about the function field inside tools?
What change do I need to make in my get_weather_tool definition so the API stops returning the missing required parameter error?
Can you show the correct JSON structure for a weather function tool in Azure OpenAI?
Why does my current tool config fail with 'Missing required parameter: tools[0].function', and what should I replace it with?
Why do I get a 404 with toyaikit when I try Groq or another non-OpenAI model?
answer_llm:
You get the 404 because `toyaikit`’s `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` uses OpenAI’s **Responses API** (`responses.create`), and that endpoint is **OpenAI-specific**. Groq and other non-OpenAI providers don’t implement it, so the request fails.

Use the **chat completions** classes instead, since they call the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that providers like Groq support.

In short:
- **Wrong for Groq/non-OpenAI:** `OpenAIResponsesRunner`
- **Use instead:** `OpenAIChatCompletionsClient` + `OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner`

For Anthropic, use `AnthropicClient` + `AnthropicMessagesRunner`.

If you want, I can show the exact code change for your current setup.
answer_orig:
If you get a `404` (or similar) when using toyaikit with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, it's because `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI's Responses endpoint (`responses.create`), which only OpenAI implements. Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

### OpenAI or Groq (chat completions)

```python
import os
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)   # functions need type hints + an Args: docstring

# OpenAI:
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

# Groq (same runner, just point the OpenAI client at Groq's base URL):
groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", client=groq_client)

runner = OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

### Anthropic

```python
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import AnthropicClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import AnthropicMessagesRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)

# Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment automatically:
llm_client = AnthropicClient(model="claude-haiku-4-5")

runner = AnthropicMessagesRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

Notes:

- `runner.loop(prompt=...)` runs one turn programmatically and returns a result with `.last_message`, `.all_messages`, and `.tokens` — no chat interface needed. Use `runner.run()` only for the interactive Jupyter loop.
- Pass an explicit, current model id. For Anthropic, `claude-haiku-4-5` / `claude-sonnet-4-5` work; an outdated id can 404.
- For Groq you'll see a harmless `UnknownModelWarning: No pricing data...` — the call still succeeds, only cost calculation is skipped.

`responses.create` is OpenAI-specific, so non-OpenAI providers should use `chat.completions.create` instead.

If you get a `404` (or similar) when using toyaikit with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, it's because `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI's Responses endpoint (`responses.create`), which only OpenAI implements. Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

### OpenAI or Groq (chat completions)

```python
import os
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)   # functions need type hints + an Args: docstring

# OpenAI:
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

# Groq (same runner, just point the OpenAI client at Groq's base URL):
groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", client=groq_client)

runner = OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

### Anthropic

```python
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import AnthropicClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import AnthropicMessagesRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)

# Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment automatically:
llm_client = AnthropicClient(model="claude-haiku-4-5")

runner = AnthropicMessagesRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

Notes:

- `runner.loop(prompt=...)` runs one turn programmatically and returns a result with `.last_message`, `.all_messages`, and `.tokens` — no chat interface needed. Use `runner.run()` only for the interactive Jupyter loop.
- Pass an explicit, current model id. For Anthropic, `claude-haiku-4-5` / `claude-sonnet-4-5` work; an outdated id can 404.
- For Groq you'll see a harmless `UnknownModelWarning: No pricing data...` — the call still succeeds, only cost calculation is skipped.

question: Why do I get a 402 error from OpenRouter when I call responses.create() in homework, and what should I change to stop it?
answer_llm:
OpenRouter can return a **402** on `responses.create()` because it checks the **maximum possible output tokens** up front. If you don’t set a reasonable `max_output_tokens`, the default can be very large, and a free/limited key may get rejected before the model runs.

### What to change
Set a lower `max_output_tokens`, for example:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

### If you’re using `rag_helper.py`
Add the same parameter in the `llm()` method.

### If you’re using ToyAIKit
Pass it via `extra_kwargs`:

```python
llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 should be enough for the homework. If it still fails, also check that your `.env` points to OpenRouter and that your key/model are valid.
answer_orig:
OpenRouter can return APIStatusError with code 402 when responses.create() is called without a reasonable max_output_tokens limit. This happens because OpenRouter bills/limits checks against the maximum possible output (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can be rejected before the model runs. This is different from a direct OpenAI endpoint (which typically returns 429 for insufficient quota).

Fix

Pass a lower limit in your responses.create() call:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI()  # uses OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL from .env

response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework with rag_helper.py, add the same parameter in the ``llm()`` method.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework Q6 with ToyAIKit:

```python
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIClient

llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 is enough for homework answers; you can raise it later if needed.

If it still fails

1) Confirm your `.env` points at OpenRouter:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

2) Check your OpenRouter key limit and remaining credits at https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

3) Prefer a pinned model (for example `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`) instead of `openrouter/free`, which can route to models with different limits.

Note: This behavior is different from OpenAI’s typical 429 handling for insufficient quota. If you still encounter issues after these steps, double-check the model and endpoint configuration to ensure the key is valid and has sufficient credits.

question: In the Module 1 homework, when people say to point RAG at the chunk index, does that mean using the split docs instead of the original ones?
answer_llm:
Yes — it means using the split documents, i.e. the chunks, not the original full documents.

So in Module 1, “point RAG at the chunk index” means you build and search the index over the chunked records, for example:

```python
index.fit(chunks)
```

not:

```python
index.fit(documents)
```

That way, RAG retrieves relevant chunks and sends only those into the prompt.
answer_orig:
It means you should build and search the index using the chunked documents, not the original full documents.

For example, if your original records are in `documents` and your split records are in `chunks`, fit the search index on `chunks`:

```python
index.fit(chunks)
```

not:

```python
index.fit(documents)
```

question: How should I count tokens for the Module 1 homework if I’m using a model provider other than OpenAI?
answer_llm:
For a non-OpenAI provider, get the token count from the model’s **response object usage fields** if they’re available.

- Check the provider’s response for something like `usage.input_tokens`, `usage.prompt_tokens`, etc.
- **Do not** use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for other providers like Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, or Groq, because tokenization differs.
- If the provider does **not** expose token usage, use that provider’s **native tokenizer** as an approximation.

If it’s a multiple-choice question, choose the closest option.

question: Why does my homework repo link get rejected with a 500 or other non-200 code, and what should I check first?
answer_llm:
The checker sends a GET request to your submitted repo URL and expects an HTTP `200` response. If it gets `500` or any other non-200, the link usually isn’t publicly reachable.

Check these first:
- Make sure the repo is **public**
- Submit the **plain repo URL**, not one ending in `.git`
- Verify there’s **no typo** in the URL

Quick test: open the link in an **incognito/private window**. If it loads there, it should work for the checker too.
answer_orig:
The submission checker fetches the URL you submit with a GET request and expects an HTTP `200` response. A non-200 status (404, 500, etc.) almost always means the link isn't publicly reachable as-is. Check the usual causes:

- **Private repo** — make the repository public so the checker can access it.
- **Trailing `.git`** — submit the plain repository URL (e.g. `https://github.com/you/repo`), not `https://github.com/you/repo.git`.
- **Typo in the URL** — paste the exact link.

A quick test: open the URL in a private/incognito browser window. If it loads for you there, it'll work for the checker too.

question: What exactly are embeddings in vector search, and why do we convert text or other non-numeric data into numbers?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are a way to convert text or other non-numeric data into numbers while preserving meaning and context.

Why do this?
- Computers are good at math, not directly at understanding text.
- By turning words, documents, or other data into vectors of numbers, we can compare them mathematically.
- Similar items should end up with similar vectors, so their proximity reflects semantic similarity.

This is what makes vector search work: instead of matching exact words, we can find items that are meaningfully similar.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: How do embeddings keep meaning and context when they turn things into numerical vectors?
answer_llm:
Embeddings keep meaning and context by turning data into vectors in a way that places **similar items close together** in numerical space.

So instead of just converting words to arbitrary numbers, an embedding algorithm is designed so that:
- similar texts produce similar vectors,
- the relative position of vectors reflects semantic similarity,
- and this lets us compare meaning using math like cosine similarity.

This is why embeddings are useful for searching and finding related content.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: Why should similar inputs produce similar vectors in an embedding model?
answer_llm: Because embeddings are supposed to preserve meaning and context. If two inputs are similar, the model should place their vectors close together in vector space so that similarity measures like cosine similarity can detect that relationship.

question: How does the closeness of embedding values help with semantic similarity search?
answer_llm: Because semantically similar items are encoded as vectors that are close together in embedding space, you can compare their numerical values and use distance or cosine similarity to find related content. In other words, similar meanings should produce similar embeddings, so closeness in the vector space helps a search system identify semantically similar documents.

question: What’s the connection between embeddings, vector space models, and dimensionality reduction?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are the numerical vectors that represent data while preserving meaning and context. The **vector space model** is the idea of representing items as points in a vector space so that similar items are close together. **Dimensionality reduction** is related because embeddings often compress complex, high-dimensional data into a smaller vector space while keeping semantic relationships intact.

So, in short:  
- **Embeddings** = the vectors  
- **Vector space model** = the representation framework  
- **Dimensionality reduction** = the process of mapping data into a lower-dimensional space while preserving meaning

question: For vector search, is it better to store only the answer, only the question, or both together in the embedding?
answer_llm:
There’s no single best choice. The course suggests treating it as an experiment on your dataset.

Common options:
- **Answer only**: often works well because the embedding captures semantic similarity.
- **Question only**: works if user queries are similar to the indexed questions.
- **Question + answer together**: often performs best, but is a bit longer and slightly more expensive.

Best practice: try all three and pick the one with the best hit rate / MRR on your ground-truth set.
answer_orig:
There's no single right answer — it's an experiment to run on your dataset. The course shows three options:

- Embed the answer (`text`) only — works because the model captures semantic similarity between questions and their answers.
- Embed the question only — works because user queries look like the indexed questions.
- Embed `question + " " + text` — often the best, but produces longer input and slightly more cost.

Pick whichever gives the best hit rate / MRR on your ground-truth set. The course materials include a side-by-side comparison.

question: What does cosine similarity actually measure between two vectors?
answer_llm: Cosine similarity measures **how aligned two vectors are**, based on the **angle between them**, not their length. If the vectors point in the same direction, similarity is high; if they’re orthogonal, it’s zero.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity is a measure used to calculate the similarity between two non-zero vectors, often used in text analysis to determine how similar two documents are based on their content. This metric computes the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which are typically word counts or TF-IDF values of the documents. The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

question: How is cosine similarity used when comparing documents or text?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity compares documents by turning them into vectors and measuring the angle between those vectors. If the vectors point in a similar direction, the documents are considered more similar in content.

In practice:
- text is converted into vectors, often using word counts, TF-IDF, or embeddings
- cosine similarity is computed from those vectors
- a value near 1 means the documents are very similar, 0 means little or no similarity

If the vectors are normalized to unit length, cosine similarity can be computed efficiently as a dot product.

question: Why do we use the angle between vectors instead of raw values in cosine similarity?
answer_llm:
Because cosine similarity is meant to compare **direction**, not size.

Using the **angle between vectors** makes the similarity independent of the vectors’ magnitudes. That way, two vectors with the same pattern but different raw values can still be considered highly similar. When vectors are normalized to unit length, cosine similarity becomes the dot product, which is why it’s efficient to compute.

question: Why does cosine similarity become just a dot product once the vectors are normalized?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity becomes a dot product when the vectors are normalized because the vector lengths no longer matter.

Cosine similarity is:

\[
\cos(\theta) = \frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\|\|b\|}
\]

If both vectors are normalized to unit length, then:

\[
\|a\| = 1,\quad \|b\| = 1
\]

So the formula simplifies to:

\[
\cos(\theta) = a \cdot b
\]

That’s why, once embeddings are normalized, you can compute cosine similarity just by taking the dot product.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In this case, cosine similarity is equivalent to simply taking the dot product between the query and each document embedding. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: How can matrix multiplication be used to compare one query against many embeddings at once?
answer_llm:
If the embeddings are normalized, you can stack all document embeddings into a matrix and multiply it by the query vector. That computes the dot product between the query and each embedding at once, which is equivalent to cosine similarity.

So instead of comparing the query to each embedding one by one, matrix multiplication gives you all similarity scores in a single operation.

This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: If embeddings are all unit length, why don't we need to divide by their magnitudes anymore?
answer_llm:
If embeddings are all unit length, their magnitudes are all 1, so dividing by the magnitude does nothing.

That’s why cosine similarity simplifies to just the dot product:

\[
\cos(\theta) = \frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\|\|b\|}
\]

If \(\|a\| = \|b\| = 1\), then:

\[
\cos(\theta) = a \cdot b
\]

So no extra division is needed.

question: What makes cosine similarity efficient for vector search after normalization?
answer_llm: After normalization, cosine similarity becomes just a dot product. That makes vector search efficient because you can compute all similarities with a single matrix multiplication instead of calculating angles or vector magnitudes separately.

This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: Why is cosine similarity basically the same as multiplying the embedding matrix by the query vector?
answer_llm:
Because if the embeddings and the query are **normalized to unit length**, cosine similarity becomes just the **dot product**.

So for each document embedding vector, you compute:

- `embedding · query`

and doing that for all documents at once is exactly:

- `embedding_matrix @ query_vector`

That’s why it looks like “multiplying the embedding matrix by the query vector” — it’s just a fast way to compute cosine similarity for every vector in the matrix.

question: For Module 2, do I have to create a whole new GitHub repository, or can I reuse the one from Module 1 and just start another codespace?

question: Why do the dot-product version and the loop version of vector search return slightly different scores?
answer_llm:
They can differ slightly because of **floating-point precision** and **different summation order**.

- The dot-product/matrix version uses optimized BLAS code.
- The loop version adds the numbers sequentially in Python.
- Floating-point addition is not perfectly associative, so the last few decimal places may change.

The math is the same, so the scores are not really wrong. Compare them with a tolerance, for example:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```
answer_orig:
In [Lesson 4 (Vector Search)](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/02-vector-search/lessons/04-vector-search.md) we compute the scores two ways — `scores = X.dot(v_query)` and the equivalent for-loop — and they can come out slightly different. This is normal, it's just floating-point precision. If you compare them with `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` you might get `False`, even though both compute the same dot products. They just add the numbers up in a slightly different order, and floating-point addition isn't perfectly associative, so the results can differ in the last few decimal places.

Under the hood, `X.dot(v)` runs optimized BLAS code that sums in a different order than a sequential Python loop. The math is the same; only the rounding differs, so the values aren't really "wrong."

Just compare them with a small tolerance instead of expecting an exact match:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

question: Is it normal if `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` says `False` even when both methods look equivalent?
answer_llm:
Yes, that can be normal.

`X.dot(v_query)` and the for-loop version compute the same dot products, but they may add numbers in a different order. Because floating-point arithmetic is not perfectly associative, the results can differ slightly in the last decimal places, so `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` may return `False` if the default tolerance is too strict.

Try using a small tolerance, for example:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

The values are not really wrong; they’re just numerically a bit different.

Just compare them with a small tolerance instead of expecting an exact match:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

question: Are those tiny differences in vector search results a bug, or just floating-point rounding?
answer_llm:
Yes — those tiny differences are just floating-point rounding, not a bug.

In vector search, `X.dot(v_query)` and a for-loop compute the same dot products, but they may add numbers in a different order. Because floating-point addition isn’t perfectly associative, the results can differ slightly in the last few decimal places.

Use a tolerance check like:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

So the values are effectively the same, even if they don’t match exactly.

question: Why am I getting IndexError: list index out of range when I try to grab the best chunk in vector search?
answer_llm:
The error usually means your embeddings and chunks are not aligned.

Check that you generate embeddings from the **same chunk list** you later search over:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

`len(X)` should match `len(chunks)`. If they don’t match, trying to access the “best” chunk can produce `IndexError: list index out of range`.

Also, in the homework/RAG setup, make sure you’re indexing the **chunks**, not the original full documents.

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

The number of rows in `X` should be equal to `len(chunks)`.

question: Why doesn’t my Q2 cosine similarity match any multiple-choice option in Module 2 homework?
answer_llm:
Most likely because you used a different embedding model than the homework expects.

For Module 2 Homework, you should use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py`, not `sentence-transformers`:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

If you used something like `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` or `all-mpnet-base-v2`, your cosine similarity can fall outside the multiple-choice options.

Also check that you are:

- embedding the page’s `content` field, not the filename or whole row
- comparing against the Q1 query vector
- using normalized vectors; with `Embedder`, the dot product is already the cosine similarity

If it’s still slightly off after that, choose the closest option, since small numerical differences are expected.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is using a different embedding model than the homework specifies. Homework 2 tells you **not** to use `sentence-transformers` and to use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py` instead:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

Both approaches produce the same vectors for the same model, but if you embed with a different model (for example the `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` model from the lessons, or `all-mpnet-base-v2`) you'll get a cosine value that isn't among the options.

Also check that:

- You're embedding the page's `content` field (not the filename or the whole dict).
- You're comparing against the query vector from Q1.
- The vectors are normalized — the `Embedder` returns normalized vectors, so the dot product is the cosine similarity directly.

If your value is still slightly off after using the right model, pick the closest option — small numerical differences are expected.

question: How do I set up Gemini API keys so Kestra can use them in the orchestration module?
answer_llm:
Set them as environment variables **before starting Kestra**.

For Gemini, you need:

- `GEMINI_API_KEY` = the plain API key, used by the AI Copilot
- `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` = the same key, but **base64-encoded**, used by flows

Example:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)
```

Then restart Kestra so it picks up the variables:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

Inside a flow, reference it without the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

Important: the Copilot uses the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY`, while the orchestration flows use `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`.
answer_orig:
Kestra reads secrets from environment variables that are prefixed with `SECRET_` and whose value is **base64-encoded**. You export them in the terminal *before* starting Kestra. For Gemini you need two variables — the plain one (used by the AI Copilot) and the base64-encoded `SECRET_` one (used by the flows):

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # used by AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)             # used by the flows
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for flow 3
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for web search (flows 3, 5, 6)
```

Then start (or restart) Kestra so it picks up the variables:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

Inside a flow, reference a secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

See the [setup lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/03-setup.md) for the full walkthrough. Never commit your keys to Git.

question: Why does Kestra AI Copilot keep saying it can only help create Kestra flows, and what should I change to make it work?
answer_llm:
Kestra AI Copilot is usually saying that because it **didn’t get a valid Gemini API key** and is falling back to a default refusal message.

What to change:

- Use the **plain** environment variable **`GEMINI_API_KEY`**
- Do **not** rely only on `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` for the Copilot
- Export the key **before starting Kestra**, then restart Kestra

Example:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

If it still doesn’t work, check that:

- `echo $GEMINI_API_KEY` shows the key in the same shell
- the key is valid and freshly generated
- you haven’t hit Gemini free-tier quota/rate limits

In short: **set `GEMINI_API_KEY` (plain text), restart Kestra, and keep `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` for the flows if needed.**
answer_orig:
This message means the AI Copilot didn't get a valid Gemini API key, so it falls back to a canned refusal. In Kestra's Open Source edition the Copilot only supports Gemini, and it reads the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` variable (not the base64-encoded `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` that the flows use).

Make sure you exported the plain key before starting Kestra, then restart it:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

If it still fails, the key is usually missing, mistyped, or rate-limited:

- Confirm the variable is actually set in the shell you ran `docker compose up` from (`echo $GEMINI_API_KEY`).
- Generate a fresh key in [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/app/apikey).
- If you've been running the agent/multi-agent flows a lot, you may have hit the free-tier quota (`429 Resource Exhausted`) — wait a minute and retry.

Note that the Copilot needs the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` while the flows need `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` — export both.

answer_llm:

So if you only set the `SECRET_` one for flows, the Copilot can still refuse. Export both:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Then restart Kestra:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

If it still refuses, the plain key may be missing, mistyped, or rate-limited.

question: What’s the difference between the plain Gemini key and the base64 secret key in Kestra setup?
answer_llm:
The plain `GEMINI_API_KEY` is for the **AI Copilot**.  
The `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` is the **base64-encoded secret** that Kestra flows use.

So:

- `GEMINI_API_KEY` = normal raw API key
- `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` = same key, but base64-encoded for Kestra secrets

In flows, you reference it as:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

Kestra reads the `SECRET_` env var, strips the prefix, and decodes the base64 value.

question: Why does Kestra say my Gemini key is missing or invalid even though I set SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY?
answer_llm:
Kestra expects the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` for the AI Copilot, not just `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`.

`SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` is the **base64-encoded secret** used by flows, so if you only set that, the Copilot can still say the key is missing/invalid.

Use both:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
docker compose up -d
```

Also make sure:

- you start Kestra from the same shell where the variables are set,
- you restart Kestra after exporting,
- the base64 value wasn’t corrupted by a trailing newline.

If you want, I can also show the exact `.env` setup.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is a corrupted base64 value. If you encode the key with a trailing space or newline, the decoded secret is wrong and authentication fails.

Use `echo -n` (which omits the trailing newline) and quote the value:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Other things to check:

- You must **restart** Kestra after exporting, so the container picks up the new variables: `docker compose up -d` (or `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`).
- Reference the secret in flows **without** the `SECRET_` prefix: `{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}`.
- Export the variables in the same shell session you start Kestra from.

A cleaner alternative is to put the keys in a `.env` file and pass it explicitly:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

question: Could the problem be my base64 encoding if the secret works in shell but fails in the flow?
answer_llm:
Yes — the most likely issue is the base64 encoding.

If you encoded the secret with a trailing space or newline, the decoded value in Kestra can be wrong, so it may work in your shell but fail in the flow. Use `echo -n` to avoid adding a newline:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Also make sure:

- you restart Kestra after exporting the variable
- you reference it in the flow as `{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}` without the `SECRET_` prefix
- the variable is exported in the same shell session used to start Kestra

If you want, I can help you check the exact command you used.

question: Why do we need an orchestrator like Kestra instead of just using a notebook for the whole workflow?
answer_llm:
We need an orchestrator like Kestra when the workflow is more than “run this notebook once.”

Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but real AI workflows often need:

- scheduling
- retries
- monitoring
- secret management
- chaining tasks reliably

Kestra provides those orchestration features.

In the course, Kestra is also used to show AI in production-style workflows, such as:

- AI Copilot to generate flows from natural language
- RAG to ground responses in real data
- AI agents that choose which tools to call

So the main reason is: notebooks are fine for experimentation, but an orchestrator is better for reliable, repeatable, production-like workflows.
answer_orig:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but real AI workflows need more than a script that runs once: scheduling, retries, monitoring, secret management, and reliably chaining tasks together. That's what an orchestrator like Kestra provides.

In this module Kestra is also the vehicle for the AI techniques the course is teaching: AI Copilot to generate flows from natural language, RAG to ground responses in real data, and AI agents that decide which tools to call. The goal is to see how AI fits into production-style workflows, not just notebook cells.

Kestra's AI plugins also work with any major provider (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and more), so you can swap providers in a flow without changing anything else. See the [module intro](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/01-intro.md) for the full motivation.

question: Isn’t a notebook enough if I only want to run my AI code once, or are there things it can’t handle well?
answer_llm:
A notebook is fine for learning, experimenting, or running something once.

But for real AI workflows, it often falls short because it doesn’t handle well:

- scheduling
- retries
- monitoring
- secret management
- reliably chaining multiple tasks together

So if you only want a one-off experiment, a notebook can be enough. If you want something more production-like or repeatable, an orchestrator like Kestra is better.

question: Do I have to use Kestra for the Zoomcamp, or is it only for the orchestration module?
answer_llm: No — Kestra is only required for Module 3 (Orchestration) and its homework. For the rest of the Zoomcamp, and for the capstone project, you can use another orchestrator or even none at all.
answer_orig:

A plain Python script that ingests and indexes your data is enough for full points on the ingestion-pipeline criterion (a Jupyter notebook with the same steps is worth 1 point instead of 2). You swap the provider block in a flow without changing anything else. See the [full list of supported providers](https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-ai/provider).

The course uses Gemini because it has a generous free tier, but you are free to use any provider. The [awesome-llms list](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo tracks free and free-tier options; Groq is a popular choice because it is OpenAI-compatible and works with both the chat completions and responses APIs.

There are two ways to use AI in Kestra:

- The **AI plugin** (`io.kestra.plugin.ai`) is the generic one. It is the most flexible for switching providers, though new vendor-specific API features take a bit longer to land here.
- The **provider-specific plugins** (e.g. `plugin-gemini`, `plugin-openai`) expose features unique to that vendor, such as Gemini video generation, before they reach the generic AI plugin.

For OpenAI-compatible providers that don't have their own plugin (DeepSeek, Groq, xAI), point the OpenAI plugin at the provider's base URL instead of the default endpoint.

question: If I switch from Gemini to another model in a Kestra flow, do I need to rewrite the whole workflow or just change the provider settings?
answer_llm:
You usually only need to change the provider settings, not rewrite the whole workflow.

In Kestra, the AI plugins are designed to work with different providers, so you can swap models/providers by updating things like:

- `provider.type`
- `modelName` / `model-name`
- API key
- `base_url` if needed

The course context also says you can switch providers in a flow without changing anything else, as long as the new provider is supported. However, if the new provider has different response formats or tool schemas, you may need to adapt some code.

So: **mostly just change the provider settings; full workflow rewrites are usually not needed.**

- **DeepSeek**: same idea — set the OpenAI client to DeepSeek’s OpenAI-compatible base URL.

- `api_key = your provider key`
- `base_url = provider’s OpenAI-compatible endpoint`
- `model = provider-specific model name`

question: Is there a way to build a Kestra flow without hand-writing YAML or any code at all?
answer_llm:
Yes. Kestra has no-code options that let you build a flow without hand-writing YAML or code:

- the **no-code form editor**
- the **AI Copilot** that generates flows from natural language
- **external agent skills** like Claude Code, Codex, or Kestra CTL

So the answer is **yes**, you can create a flow without manually writing YAML.
answer_orig:
Yes. You have three options that require no hand-written YAML or code:

- **The no-code form editor** builds a flow from a form instead of YAML. It is form-based (not drag-and-drop like n8n), so it suits people who don't want to edit YAML directly.
- **The AI Copilot** generates a flow from a natural-language description, and you can iterate with follow-up messages to add tasks or change the order. See the [AI Copilot lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/04-ai-copilot.md).
- **External agent skills** (Claude Code, Codex, Kestra CTL) let you generate flows and push them into Kestra from your editor without touching the Kestra UI.

Unlike Airflow, where business logic and orchestration logic are intertwined in Python, Kestra keeps the two separate: your Python script stays a plain script, and the workflow YAML just describes how and when to run it. This is closer to a cron job than to a traditional Airflow DAG.

For simple point-to-point automations (e.g. "post a Slack message when a YouTube video goes live"), a tool like Zapier or n8n may be simpler and cheaper. Kestra's advantage is observability, retries, and handling more complex or technical workflows in one place.

question: Why does ChatGPT sometimes invent Kestra YAML fields like bucket or name that Kestra doesn't accept?
answer_llm:
Because generic AI assistants are **not grounded in the exact Kestra plugin schema for your running version**. They often generate *plausible* field names that sound right, but Kestra may not accept them.

For example, the assistant might invent:

```yaml
bucket: "my-bucket"
name: "path/to/file"
```

when the real Kestra field is:

```yaml
to: "gs://my-bucket/path/to/file"
```

This happens because plugin property names can vary by plugin version, and the AI may not know the exact version-specific schema.

How to avoid it:
- **Cross-check with the official Kestra plugin docs**
- **Use Kestra’s built-in AI Copilot**, which is grounded in the current schema
- **Validate/test the YAML in your Kestra environment**

If you share the YAML snippet, I can help check whether the fields are valid.
answer_orig:
A general AI (e.g., ChatGPT) isn't grounded in Kestra's plugin schemas for your running version, so it can surface plausible but invalid property names (such as `bucket`/`name`) instead of the real, version-specific property. For example:

**GCS Upload** — invented `bucket`/`name` props vs. the real `to` property:

```yaml
# Incorrect (invented props)
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  bucket: "my-bucket"
  name: "path/to/file"

# Correct
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  to: "gs://my-bucket/path/to/file"
```

**BigQuery LoadFromGcs** — split `projectId`/`dataset`/`table` vs. the real `destinationTable`:

```yaml
# Incorrect (pseudo-split properties)
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  projectId: "my-project"
  dataset: "my_dataset"
  table: "my_table"

# Correct
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  destinationTable: "my-project.my_dataset.my_table"
```

To avoid this:

- Cross-check generated YAML against the official plugin docs: https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-gcp
- Use Kestra's built-in AI Copilot, which is grounded in the current plugin schema for your running version.
- Validate and test your YAML in your Kestra environment to ensure it parses and runs as expected.

Note that plugin properties can vary by plugin version; what's correct in one release may be invalid in another. If you share a snippet, we can help verify it against the docs.

question: What is the correct way to write the GCS upload step in Kestra without using fake bucket/name fields?

Use `to` with the full GCS URI:

```yaml

  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  to: "gs://my-bucket/path/to/file"
```

Avoid `bucket` and `name` — those are fake/invented fields for this plugin version.

```yaml
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  to: "gs://my-bucket/path/to/file"
```

**BigQuery LoadFromGcs** — split `projectId`/`dataset`/`table` vs. the real `destinationTable`:

```yaml

# Correct
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  destinationTable: "my-project.my_dataset.my_table"
```

To avoid this:

- Cross-check generated YAML against the official plugin docs: https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-gcp
- Use Kestra's built-in AI Copilot, which is grounded in the current plugin schema for your running version.
- Validate and test your YAML in your Kestra environment to ensure it parses and runs as expected.

Note that plugin properties can vary by plugin version; what's correct in one release may be invalid in another. If you share a snippet, we can help verify it against the docs.

question: Why do we bother tracking token usage in Kestra flows?
answer_llm:
Because in Kestra flows, token usage maps directly to what LLM providers charge for. Tracking it helps you:

- control and forecast cost,
- optimize prompts by reducing unnecessary tokens,
- and set guardrails/alerts so scheduled or looping flows don’t run up a surprise bill.

You can surface token counts from the model response in logs, metrics, or dashboards.
answer_orig:
Token usage is tracked because it maps directly to what LLM providers charge you for, and Kestra workflows run LLM calls on a schedule or in loops where costs add up fast. The main reasons:

- **Cost control**: tokens are the unit providers bill by, so tracking usage lets you budget, forecast, and spot cost spikes before they get expensive.
- **Prompt optimization**: seeing how many tokens each prompt and response consumes helps you tighten prompts and trim output without losing quality.
- **Guardrails**: in long-running or looping flows, usage monitoring lets you set thresholds and alerts so a runaway task doesn't quietly rack up a large bill.

In practice, each AI task in a Kestra flow can capture usage from the provider's response (for example `usage.total_tokens`), and you can surface those numbers in logs, metrics, or dashboards to keep an eye on spend per run.

question: Why am I getting a Docker Desktop error that says a path under /tmp is not shared from the host when I start Kestra?
answer_llm:
This usually means Docker Desktop is not allowed to access the host path Kestra is trying to mount, often something like `/tmp/kestra-wd`.

Fix it by sharing that path in Docker Desktop:

1. Open **Docker Desktop**
2. Click the **gear icon** in the top right
3. Go to **Resources > File Sharing**
4. Click **+**
5. Add `/tmp` or the specific path, like `/tmp/kestra-wd`
6. Click **Apply & restart**

After Docker restarts, run `docker compose up -d` again.
answer_orig:

1. Open Docker Desktop.
2. Click the gear icon (Settings) in the top right.
3. Go to **Resources > File Sharing**.
4. Click the **+** (Add) button.
5. Enter `/tmp` (or the specific path like `/tmp/kestra-wd`) and press Enter.
6. Click **Apply & restart**.

Once Docker restarts, rerun `docker compose up -d` to start Kestra.

question: Why am I getting a 404 NOT_FOUND from Gemini in the Kestra flow, and how can I fix it?
answer_llm:
A `404 NOT_FOUND` from Gemini in a Kestra flow usually means the **model id in the flow is not available to your account anymore** or has changed over time.

### How to fix it
- Open the affected YAML flow.
- Replace the Gemini `modelName` with a model that is currently available to you.
- For example:

```yaml
provider:
  type: io.kestra.plugin.ai.provider.GoogleGemini
  modelName: gemini-3.5-flash
  apiKey: "{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}"
```

- If you set the model in `docker-compose.yml`, update the equivalent `model-name` there too.
- Then rerun the flow, or restart the Docker Compose services if you changed the config.

### Important
Don’t assume the original model was permanently shut down — model availability can vary by account and change over time. Check the official Gemini model list for current model ids:
https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/models
answer_orig:

```yaml
provider:
  type: io.kestra.plugin.ai.provider.GoogleGemini
  modelName: gemini-3.5-flash
  apiKey: "{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}"
```

In `docker-compose.yml`, update the equivalent `model-name` setting. Then rerun the flow, or restart the Docker Compose services if you changed their configuration.

Check the [official Gemini model list](https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/models) for current model ids. Do not assume the original model was permanently shut down based on a temporary `404`; select a model that the API makes available to your account.

question: For the Module 3 Kestra homework, what exactly am I supposed to upload or link in the submission form if most of the work was done in the Kestra UI?
answer_llm:
You should upload the **Kestra flow YAML files** you created or edited for the homework to a **public GitHub repo** (or another code-hosting site), then paste that repo link into the submission form.

What to include:
- The **flow YAML files** exported from Kestra
- Any **scripts** those flows call, if you modified or wrote them
- A short **README** is optional, but not required

Suggested structure:
- `module-3/flows/your-flow.yml`

Important:
- The **form answers** for Q1, Q2, etc. go directly into the submission form fields
- The repo link is just for the **flow files/code**

So even if most of the work was done in the Kestra UI, you still submit the **downloaded flow definitions** from Kestra in a public repo.
answer_orig:
The submission form asks for a link to a public repo (GitHub or any code-hosting site), but unlike earlier modules the work happens inside the Kestra UI rather than as code you write from scratch. That leaves people unsure what to put in the repo.

Download the Kestra flows you created or edited for the homework and commit them to the repo. The flow YAML files are the code you submit. Put the flows in a folder (e.g. `module-3/flows/`), make the repo public, and paste the link in the submission form. The form answers (Q1, Q2, etc.) are separate and go directly into the form fields.
